title: COP28: Tracking the Energy Outcomes – Topics - IEA
gist: IEA's COP28 tracker shows most 2030 energy pledges running behind schedule despite record renewables and EV growth: efficiency gains are a quarter of target pace, coal power hit an all-time high, CO2 emissions set a new record, and clean energy investment outside China remains lacklustre even as fossil fuel subsidies swing wildly with prices.

themes.theme: Renewables tripling falls short of pace

insights.claim: In 2024 roughly 700 GW of new renewable capacity was added globally, marking the 22nd consecutive record year for renewables installations.
insights.speaker: IEA
insights.so_what: Confirms renewables buildout momentum is real and accelerating, relevant for equipment makers and utility capex cycles even though it isn't yet on the tripling trajectory.
insights.quote: Around 700 gigawatts (GW) of total renewable capacity was added globally.
insights.locator: Triple renewable energy capacity globally by 2030

insights.claim: Despite record annual additions, global renewable power capacity is only on track to grow about 2.5x from 2022 levels by 2030, short of the COP28 pledge to triple capacity, meaning deployment must still accelerate materially.
insights.speaker: IEA
insights.so_what: A structural gap between current trajectory (2.5x) and the policy target (3x) implies continued policy support and financing incentives are likely, not a plateau in renewables demand.
insights.locator: Triple renewable energy capacity globally by 2030

themes.theme: Efficiency stalls, coal hits a record before its projected peak

insights.claim: Meeting the COP28 goal of doubling energy efficiency improvements requires average annual gains above 4% from a 2022 baseline through 2030, but energy intensity improved by only 1% in 2024.
insights.speaker: IEA
insights.so_what: A 4x shortfall versus the required pace signals efficiency-linked policy (standards, retrofits, industrial electrification) is an underexploited lever and a laggard relative to renewables and EVs.
insights.quote: According to the latest IEA data, energy intensity – a key measure of efficiency – improved by just 1% in 2024.
insights.locator: Double the global rate of energy efficiency improvements by 2030

insights.claim: Global electricity generation from coal reached an all-time high in 2024, even as current policy settings point to coal demand peaking before the end of the decade and then declining gradually.
insights.speaker: IEA
insights.so_what: A near-term coal peak under existing policy (not accelerated) suggests coal demand destruction will be gradual absent new policy tightening, a relevant base case for coal miners and thermal utilities.
insights.quote: coal demand is projected to peak before the end of this decade and decline gradually
insights.locator: Accelerate efforts to phase down unabated coal power

insights.claim: Energy-related CO2 emissions rose to a record high in 2024, even though strong clean energy growth has limited the pace of annual emissions increases in recent years.
insights.speaker: IEA
insights.so_what: Emissions setting new records despite clean energy growth shows clean energy additions are so far offsetting, not displacing, fossil demand growth — a key data point against a 'peak emissions' narrative.
insights.quote: these emissions still rose to a record high in 2024
insights.locator: Accelerate efforts globally towards net zero emission energy systems well before or by around mid-century

themes.theme: Fossil fuel transition and the investment sequencing risk

insights.claim: On a pathway consistent with net zero by 2050, global fossil fuel demand would need to decline substantially from 2022 levels by the end of this decade, enabled by rapid clean energy growth.
insights.speaker: IEA
insights.so_what: Sets the quantitative bar IEA uses to judge whether current oil/gas demand trends are compatible with stated climate pledges — a benchmark for gauging policy credibility versus company production plans.
insights.locator: Transition away from fossil fuels in energy systems…

insights.claim: Clean energy investment in emerging and developing economies (excluding China) is currently lacklustre, and needs to triple from 2022 levels by 2030 (including China) to stay on a net-zero pathway.
insights.speaker: IEA
insights.so_what: Highlights a concentration risk: global clean energy investment narratives are skewed by China, while EM/developing economies outside China are being left behind — a gap for development finance and IFC-style blended finance vehicles to fill.
insights.quote: clean energy investment in emerging and developing economies is lacklustre outside of China
insights.locator: Transition away from fossil fuels in energy systems…

insights.claim: IEA frames an orderly energy transition as requiring careful sequencing between scaling up clean energy investment and winding down fossil fuel supply investment, warning that poor sequencing risks damaging price spikes or stranded assets.
insights.speaker: IEA
insights.so_what: Gives investors a explicit risk framework: premature underinvestment in fossil fuel supply ahead of demand decline is flagged by IEA itself as a driver of price spikes, relevant to upstream oil & gas capex debates.
insights.quote: it is important to sequence the scaling up of investment in clean energy and the decline in investment in fossil fuel supplies in order to avoid damaging price spikes or stranded assets
insights.locator: Transition away from fossil fuels in energy systems…

themes.theme: Energy access and jobs shift more than they shrink

insights.claim: Global electricity access has grown by more than a billion people since 2010, but the number of people without electricity increased for the first time in 2022 due to the Covid-19 pandemic and the global energy crisis, before progress resumed in 2023-2024.
insights.speaker: IEA
insights.so_what: Shows energy-crisis price shocks can reverse decade-long social progress on electrification, a reminder that affordability shocks have direct humanitarian, not just market, consequences.
insights.quote: the impacts of the Covid-19 pandemic and global energy crisis led the number of people without electricity to increase for the first time in 2022
insights.locator: … in a just, orderly and equitable manner

insights.claim: More than 2 billion people still rely on polluting fuels and technologies for cooking, and the world remains off track to achieve universal clean cooking access by 2030.
insights.speaker: IEA
insights.so_what: A durable, large addressable population underserved by clean cooking technology represents both a policy financing gap and a long-run demand pool for LPG, biogas, and electric cooking solutions.
insights.quote: More than 2 billion people still using polluting fuels and technologies for cooking
insights.locator: … in a just, orderly and equitable manner

insights.claim: Clean energy sector jobs rose by 1.5 million in 2023, and on a COP28-aligned pathway the global energy sector would employ roughly 25% more workers in 2030 than in 2023, though jobs will shift location and skill requirements.
insights.speaker: IEA
insights.so_what: Net job growth masks reallocation risk — workforce and regional dislocation from fossil fuel to clean energy jobs implies retraining and just-transition financing needs, a policy and labor-market watch item.
insights.quote: the global energy sector employs close to 25% more workers in 2030 than it did in 2023
insights.locator: … in a just, orderly and equitable manner

themes.theme: Low-carbon technology scale-up and the methane gap

insights.claim: Low-emissions hydrogen and CCUS capacity (including direct air capture) need to rise strongly on a COP28-aligned pathway, and nuclear power, though a mature technology, still needs strong capacity growth by 2030 to meet mid-century net zero goals.
insights.speaker: IEA
insights.so_what: Positions hydrogen, CCUS, and nuclear as the technologies with the largest gap between current deployment and required scale-up, useful for identifying where policy support and capital are most likely to concentrate next.

insights.claim: Energy-sector methane emissions have stayed above 120 million tonnes annually, held up by record global production of oil, gas and coal and limited mitigation to date, even as monitoring and data collection improve.
insights.speaker: IEA
insights.so_what: Ties methane leakage directly to record fossil fuel production volumes rather than to a lack of monitoring technology, implying methane abatement is a production-side operational and regulatory issue, not just a measurement gap.
insights.quote: Record global production of oil, gas and coal – along with limited mitigation efforts to date – have kept methane emissions from the energy sector worldwide above 120 million tonnes annually.

themes.theme: EV adoption surges while fossil subsidies swing with prices

insights.claim: Global electric car sales rose more than 25% in 2024, surpassing 17 million units and accounting for one-fifth of all car sales, while roughly one in ten two- and three-wheelers on the road globally is now electric.
insights.speaker: IEA
insights.so_what: EVs already at one-fifth of global car sales confirms electrification is the leading decarbonization lever in road transport, relevant for oil demand forecasts and battery/EV supply chain positioning.
insights.quote: Global sales of electric cars rose by over 25% in 2024, surpassing 17 million units and accounting for one-fifth of all car sales.
insights.locator: Accelerate the reduction of emissions from road transport

insights.claim: Fossil fuel subsidies spiked in the 2022 energy crisis, with natural gas and electricity consumption subsidies more than doubling versus 2021 and oil subsidies rising about 85%, before total subsidy value declined in 2023 as some provisions expired and global prices pulled back from record highs.
insights.speaker: IEA
insights.so_what: Shows fossil fuel subsidies behave as a price-linked automatic stabilizer that swells during crises and recedes with prices, meaning subsidy levels are a lagging signal of energy-price stress rather than a stable structural feature to bet against.
insights.locator: Phase out inefficient fossil fuel subsidies

themes.theme: Next-round climate diplomacy and NDC ambition

insights.claim: Countries are now developing the second cycle of Nationally Determined Contributions (NDCs) under the Paris Agreement following the first Global Stocktake at COP28, and the ambition and implementation of these NDCs will determine the pace of global emissions decline over the next decade.
insights.speaker: IEA
insights.so_what: The upcoming NDC cycle is flagged by IEA as the pivotal near-term policy event shaping emissions trajectories — a calendar catalyst to watch for shifts in national climate and energy policy.
insights.quote: Strong ambition and implementation of these NDCs will be crucial in determining the pace at which global greenhouse gas emissions decline over the next decade.
insights.locator: Raising the bar for the next round of NDCs

insights.claim: IEA is convening a third series of High-Level Energy Transition Dialogues in partnership with the Brazilian COP30 Presidency, aimed at advancing NDC ambition and boosting financing for secure and just energy transitions, building on similar dialogues ahead of COP28 and COP29.
insights.speaker: IEA
insights.so_what: Signals an institutionalized diplomatic channel (public-private dialogues) that has directly shaped prior COP outcomes, worth tracking ahead of COP30 for early signals on financing commitments and NDC ambition.
insights.locator: Working with countries to accelerate progress
